One thing all of the sequels got wrong about the Predator is that they are supposed to be extremely skilled hunters. One important trait of a hunter is patience. In the original movie, the Predator would stalk and terrorize the soldiers without being properly seen until the end. Predator 2 was the movie that introduced the taunting and arrogant nature of them. I understand that they kill to test themselves and making the enemy angry probably makes things more interesting but i always saw the Predator as an "honorable" creature. They left multiple people alive during the movies and even showed great respect to those who defeated their kind so why portray them as genocidal maniacs? I hope the movie has more to it than the trailer.

NO ARNOLD?!? NOO!!  Predator is nothing without Arnold. "Go to the choppa!"  This would make the great sequel: Arnold is a high school principal retiring from a school district. All of a sudden weeks before his retirement, the predators wrist gizmo, that Arnold kept as souvenir from 1987 Predator, signals back to the aliens.  So Predator returns, but theyre not out for everyone's blood, but freshmen women.  So the Predator starts killing off boyfriends, stalkers, pedos, anyone blocking its path to kidnapping female students and sending them all to a hive, or cave, for mating.  Arnold goes to work in saving the captured women before they become victims to alien devices.  He fights the predator with tactics learned from the old film, freeing trapped students, and eventually killing off the dominant predator.   Now that would be a good sequel!

Couldn't agree more.  That's exactly what I was thinking while watching this.  Part of what made the original film work so well, I think, was the fact that it felt sort of suffocating.  A small group of characters in a remote, thick, suffocating environment.  It was all simple and self-contained.  The trailer for this new one makes it seems like it's gonna jump around too much and try to do too much (although I will, of course, reserve full judgement until I can actually see the movie).  I'm not saying I want to see a remake of the original (because I don't), but I think a new Predator movie that more-or-less sticks to that same kind of formula is one that I would be excited to see.  Sort of like Star Wars: The Force Awakens.  Follows many of the same beats and basic outline as the original film, but was definitely still different enough to stand on its own.  That's the new Predator film that I, personally, would like to see.

Predator was clearly an horrific primal creature with low intelligence (got trapped by boyscout tricks) and using such space hunting tech does not mean he is even able to craft it. In the first movie the creature is defined "sporty" by Dutch but it is clearly SARCASM...the woman was not the primary target in that moment because she was not armed, but she would have been just killed after Dutch. Predator has not honor at all...he has overconfidence on advanced tech to hunt stealthy and have fun on killing  peoples. Comics started all the nerdy things and we got "fancy chromed space samurais with honor code" willing to team up with humans or even accepting them in a "Yautja-KAI Dojo". Xenomorph received a similar treatment because in  Cameron movie was wiped and even defined by soldiers as a "space bug" but we all know how HARD the xeno body is...and marines are said to use special bullets to penetrate it...but NERD comic writers overplotted on few movie lines and we got the "Antlike space xeno with many colours\queens\shapes etc". A talented director like John Carpenter,Veroheven, Scott, Dante, Kubric would never accept to make an AVP movie or transform an horror movie in a silly comic action-comedy just to sell to a broad audience...but the nowadays casual dumb mass will even probably pay to watch a JAWS reboot with an upgraded superpowered shark in 4k...wtf i want 80's back

If I was going to make a new Predator movie, I would say ok here are the ground rules:
1) You can't make it about some other creature or alter the predators to be larger or more lethal - that is cheap and not how you make a movie "better".
2) It must be Rated "R".
3) No puns or one-liners.
4) We must learn something new and interesting about the Predators, but you can't reveal everything, it can't contradict what we know, and it can't be the basis of the whole plot.
5) The hero must be intelligent and not make stupid decisions.
6) It can't be "personal" - the hero is doing what they are because they are a HERO - not because they want revenge, or are trapped and have no choice, or they didn't give a crap until it was their loved one in danger. A good person to sympathize with and care about.
7) It must be suspenseful, intelligent, intense, and creative.
8) No standard predictable 'Hollywood ending'.
9) It must be scientifically feasible within the sci-fi parameters already established.
10) It must have a diversity of pacing - some slow parts, character development, interspersed with action parts. It can't just be nonstop loud noises and action from start to finish where you couldn't care less about what happens.

That’s what a predator movie is anyway?

Logan Sanders the original predator film was so effective because it had elements of mystery which grabs the interest of the viewer, with very clever set and monster design that kept the audience guessing as to what the creature was all about. 

Nowadays film makers are just milking the series dry, because the studios don’t want to take a risk with audiences. They use CGI because it is more cost-effective than a guy in a suit who can move in a more fluid manner. 

The sad part of it is, they get away with it because movie-goers are not as critical of films like this as they ought to be. It is the consumers responsibility to “vote with their dollar”, and not settle for an “okay movie” when going to the theatre. As usual, the trailer gives away far too much in regards as to what the film is about, and elements are completely spoiled (such as the giant predator reveal.) 

My point is that I am always surprised that more people are not as fed up about films like this as I am.

It looks promising.  Sterling K Brown and Thomas Jane are two powerful actors in their own right and the rest of the cast seem very entertaining.  The Alien vs Predator (2004) was my favourite and will be a hard movie to better as the legendary Lance Henriksen was on his game portraying Charles Bishop Weyland, the ailing boss who funds the original exploration led by Sigourney Weaver.  The hero or rather heroine of the story was Sanaa Lathan who managed to play a really convincing if not reluctant master guide into the dangerous landscape of Antarctica who for the first time actually earned the respect of the lizard hunters who settle for nothing less than your head, we didn't see that one coming.  But it was set against a very interesting and intriguing backdrop, the pyramid.  I guess we can only wait and see what September 14th brings.  Looking forward to it.  Till then, take it easy folks. ♥

Something interesting. 
Predators are basically a metaphor for one version of a projection of our own species onto an "alien" race. 
Like, think about it, since we have no proof of aliens. 
Technically any "Alien" we imagine from a story is only a mere projection of our own human lives superimposed onto something "unknown" but even still, it's how "WE"  imagine an alien would think and behave, and that's only based around our own psychology of what we think would interest a super being like that.

"Aliens would probably be hostile, they would probably want to take over."The only reason we think that is because we take what we know, and we adjust from there. 
We know WE would probably do that (projection) so it's not much of a stretch to imagine an alien species doing it. 
But that's just the thing, we are just assuming that an alien would have the same interests as us and that's the projection.

Making a guess based on the trailer and description because why not?

Hybrid vigor.  The Kaiju Predator is an engineered cross-breed not unlike a Liger (Lion/Tiger).  The gigantism may or may not be a deliberate effect of this.  And speaking of which, there may be other less happy "accidents" with this Extra-Large Slab of Ugly.  Like lowered resistance to microbes?  Yeah.

Of course, the thing with Predators is that they also carry nukes for self-destruct purposes.  That IS deliberate, and may be the real issue here: If "the hunt" is your culture, how do you deploy a planet-buster style of Weapon of Mass Destruction without being a coward engaged in push-button warfare?  Oh right, you breed an Unstable Extra-Large Slab of Ugly, shove a nuke up its butt, and infect it with every possible exo-disease you can scrounge, on the hunch that if the radiation release doesn't do it, the spread of randomly mutated diseases not native to planet just might.

Of course, it's overkill for what looks to be a cheap-to-generic reboot of a sci-fi-action franchise, but hey.  =))

I'm a big fan of predator. Ever since I was too young to watch it.

But I wish they change up the formula, here's my pitch:

What if... a Predator was a recurring character? Not a different predator that always loses, but a Predator that always wins..

Now before you go and write this off as stupid, think of Jason. Jason from the Friday the 13th films always comes back, and kills almost all the teens. He's essentially the main character since no one likes the teens. And that franchise was super successful.

So instead of highly advanced aliens who dedicate their lives to literally hunting aliens getting killed by any human... (danny glover.. im looking at you) make a recurring badass Predator who is tenacious as any hero. And find extremely creative ways for him to win. With maybe 1 or 2 survivors.
